  5. The bench press

The bench press

The bench press is the second lift. You lie on a bench, lower the bar to your chest, hold it still, then push it back up to straight arms.

This is a plain-English guide. The official IPF Technical Rules are the final word.

How to do it

The bench press has three signals from the referee, not two. Listen for all three.

  1. Lie on your back. Your head, shoulders and bottom must touch the bench. Your feet stay flat on the floor.
  2. Grip the bar with your thumbs wrapped around it. Your hands must be no more than 81 cm apart. You cannot use a reverse grip.
  3. Lift the bar out to straight arms, with help from the spotters if you want. Hold it still with your elbows locked.
  4. When the referee says "Start", lower the bar to your chest or stomach.
  5. Hold the bar still on your chest. When the referee says "Press", push it back up to straight arms.
  6. Hold it still at the top. When the referee says "Rack", put the bar back.

Why a bench press fails

Your bench press will not count if you:

  • do not wait for the "Start", "Press" or "Rack" signal
  • lift your head, shoulders or bottom off the bench during the lift
  • let the bar sink into your chest, or bounce it off your chest, to help the press
  • do not lower the bar far enough to touch your chest or stomach, or it touches your belt
  • let the bar move downward at any point while you press it up
  • do not push the bar all the way to straight, locked arms
  • lift your feet, or place them on the bench or its supports
  • get help from a spotter while lifting, between the referee's signals

Next: the deadlift
